metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the recovery action for `phase=rebasing` and `phase=awaiting_verified` in the Drain+recovery AC (e.g., rebasing → discard wipChain and re-select; awaiting_verified → re-enqueue into awaiting_verified state or discard and re-select).
findings.finding: The crash-recovery reconciliation decision rule is specified for only two of the four named kill-point phases. The plan says: mid-submit → Gerrit check → ack or re-drive; mid-handback → complete idempotent Autosubmit-removal. But AC5 also names `rebasing` and `awaiting_verified` as kill-points. No recovery action is specified for a restart where `recovery.json` contains `phase=rebasing` or `phase=awaiting_verified`. An executor must invent these rules.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery; AC: Crash-recovery/idempotency test

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command or test for AC4 (e.g., `docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' autolander` after artificially aging the heartbeat file) and for AC3 (e.g., a pytest that creates the sentinel file and asserts the bot's next poll sets phase='paused' without consuming work).
findings.finding: No proving command or test is specified for the HEALTHCHECK behavior (AC4) or the emergency-stop transition (AC3); an agent cannot verify these criteria are met beyond reading the code.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) and AC3 (Emergency-stop)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the willfarrell/autoheal Docker socket mount from `:ro` to read-write (no `:ro` suffix) in the compose service definition, since autoheal requires write access to POST /containers/{id}/restart to the Docker daemon.
findings.finding: The plan (via its dependency on S5a's docker-compose.yml edit) mounts the Docker socket for the willfarrell/autoheal sidecar as ':ro' (read-only), but autoheal must issue POST /containers/{id}/restart to the Docker daemon — a write syscall — to perform its core restart function. A read-only Unix socket mount silently blocks all write operations, so the autoheal sidecar will be deployed but unable to restart the autolander when it goes unhealthy. This contradicts the asserted capability.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) + S5a docker-compose.yml edit: 'willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (Docker socket :ro)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the docker-compose.yml autoheal service definition, mount the socket as `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (no `:ro`). Document the security implication (socket RW = root-equivalent) inline, consistent with the threat model already accepted for a single-instance Gerrit box.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) narrow the title/AC language to 'graceful-shutdown-safe recovery' and document that SIGKILL/OOM are out of scope, OR (b) add a reconciliation path for the no-recovery.json case (e.g., on startup with no recovery.json, re-query Gerrit + S3 for any change that matches the bot's identity and is in a terminal-but-unacknowledged state) and mandate SIGKILL in the crash-recovery test.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'crash-safe recovery' in both the title and the AC, but the recovery.json mechanism is only reachable via SIGTERM handling. True crashes — OOM kills (SIGKILL from the kernel), host reboots, or container runtime force-kills — bypass the SIGTERM handler entirely, leave no recovery.json, and leave the bot in whatever mid-phase state it was in. The crash-recovery test AC only says 'kills the bot at each phase' without mandating SIGKILL-level kill signals, so the test does not actually validate the title's crash-safe promise.
findings.location: Title; AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery; AC: Crash-recovery/idempotency test

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the crash-recovery test file (e.g. `tests/autolander/test_crash_recovery.py — ADD`) to the Files section so the automated-test AC has a named deliverable.
findings.finding: The last AC mandates 'an automated test kills the bot at each phase … and asserts the restarted instance neither double-submits nor strands a stack' but no test file appears in the Files section, leaving the criterion with no corresponding described deliverable.
findings.location: AC: 'Crash-recovery/idempotency test' and '## Files' section
findings.suggested_fix: Append to Files: `tests/autolander/test_crash_recovery.py — ADD: parameterized kill-at-phase tests (rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, mid-handback); assert no double-submit and acknowledged=true after restart.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that S5a is a declared hard dependency of S5d and that the Dockerfile.autolander EDIT is correctly sequenced after S5a creates it; if S5a has not landed, change the label to CREATE.
findings.finding: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` is labeled 'EDIT' in the Files section — implying it already exists — but the file is absent from the repo: `infra/compose/` contains only `Dockerfile.gerrit`, `Dockerfile.reviewbot`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `gerrit.config`.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` — EDIT

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal `depends_on` from S5d to S5a, OR move the `HEALTHCHECK` instruction into S5a's Dockerfile creation so the two tasks don't share an edit target without an ordering constraint.
findings.finding: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` is labeled EDIT in S5d's Files section but does not exist in the repo and is created as NEW only by sibling task S5a (`08e1-5505-d898-4192`). S5d has no `depends_on` edge to S5a — they are sibling tasks under the same parent S5, whose only `depends_on` is S2. The rubric's recursive-depends_on exception does not apply. If S5d lands before S5a, the HEALTHCHECK edit target is absent.
findings.location: Files section: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander — EDIT: the HEALTHCHECK`
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `depends_on: 08e1-5505-d898-4192` to S5d so the ordering is enforced, or (b) move the HEALTHCHECK line into S5a's Dockerfile.autolander creation (with a placeholder `CMD stat ...` that S5d's loop.py heartbeat write path makes functional), removing the cross-sibling file dependency entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one automated test for the emergency-stop flow: assert that creating the sentinel file transitions the bot to `paused` (stops consuming new changes), and removing it resumes consumption; cover both the active-chain and idle cases.
findings.finding: The emergency-stop AC (AC3) introduces a new operator-facing flow — sentinel file presence transitions the bot to `paused` and halts consumption — but no test is specified anywhere in the plan for this behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (Emergency-stop sentinel)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 or add a dedicated AC6: 'an automated test creates the sentinel file mid-poll, asserts the bot enters `paused` (no new work picked up), removes the sentinel, and asserts the bot resumes within one poll cycle.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test for the drain deadline-exceeded path: simulate a SIGTERM where the in-flight chain does not complete within the time budget, assert that recovery.json is written with all required fields (`change_id`, `chain_member_ids`, `tested_shas`, `phase`, `re_drive_count`, `acknowledged`), and that the process exits rather than blocking.
findings.finding: The drain time-bound path — 'past the deadline it writes a concrete recovery record and exits rather than blocking the redeploy' — is a new failure-mode behavior with no test covering the deadline-exceeded scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery), deadline sub-clause

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE path for `recovery.json`: specify that the bot atomically deletes (or replaces with a `{status: clean}` sentinel) the file immediately after successful reconciliation, and document what a missing vs stale-but-present file means on startup.
findings.finding: recovery.json has no defined RETIRE/cleanup path after a successful recovery reconciliation. If the file is not deleted (or overwritten) after the bot successfully reconciles, every subsequent restart will re-read it and attempt redundant re-reconciliation, risking spurious re-drives or mis-classification of already-acknowledged stacks.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — recovery.json lifecycle
findings.suggested_fix: After reconciliation completes successfully (all stacks resolved, S3 markers converged), atomically rename recovery.json to recovery.json.prev (or delete it). On startup, treat absence of recovery.json as 'clean start'; treat presence as 'needs reconciliation'. Add this lifecycle step explicitly to the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that after the restart reconciliation completes successfully (all stacks resolved to `acknowledged=true` or escalated), `recovery.json` is deleted (or renamed to `recovery.json.completed.<timestamp>`) before the bot enters its normal poll loop.
findings.finding: `recovery.json` has no specified retirement (delete or overwrite-to-empty) path after a successful reconciliation. Every subsequent clean restart will read the stale file and re-enter the recovery branch, causing spurious Gerrit + S3 reconciliation calls even for a bot that started cleanly after a graceful stop.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json + reconciles against LIVE Gerrit and S3's markers BEFORE selecting new work'
findings.suggested_fix: After the reconciliation loop completes without error, `os.unlink(recovery_path)` (or `recovery_path.rename(recovery_path.with_suffix('.done'))` for audit trail). If reconciliation raises an unrecoverable error, leave the file in place and exit so the next restart can retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency on S5c (e.g., 'Depends on: S5c `heartbeat_age_s` field in the status endpoint') and specify a contract-test or integration check that proves the field name and value are correct end-to-end.
findings.finding: The heartbeat age is described as 'served as heartbeat_age_s by S5c's status endpoint' but S5c is a sibling ticket with no explicit dependency edge in this plan; if S5c is not complete or the field is renamed, AC5 is silently broken.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat) — 'its age is served as heartbeat_age_s by S5c's status endpoint'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a submit-idempotency fence to `recovery.json` (e.g., the Gerrit change's current-revision SHA at the moment `POST /submit` was issued) and specify that the restart reconciler checks Gerrit's merge status AND compares `current_revision` against the fenced value before deciding to re-drive, so a post-crash Gerrit completion is correctly recognised as 'already submitted'.
findings.finding: The `submitting`-phase recovery path ("not merged? re-drive") contains a TOCTOU race that defeats the double-submit safety guarantee: if Gerrit completed the submit AFTER the crash but BEFORE the restart's Gerrit status check, the reconciler will observe 'not merged' during the race window and re-drive a second submit. The recovery.json schema includes `tested_shas` but no submit-idempotency fence (e.g., Gerrit change current-revision at submit time), so the reconciler cannot distinguish 'submit completed post-crash' from 'submit never started'.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery (RELIABILITY FALLBACK) — 'a stack that was mid-submit is checked against Gerrit (already merged? then ack; not merged? re-drive)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the recovery.json schema with a `submitted_revision` field (the patchset SHA passed to POST /submit). On restart, if `phase == 'submitting'` and the change's `current_revision` in Gerrit matches `submitted_revision` AND `status == MERGED`, treat as acknowledged. If `status == NEW/OPEN` AND `current_revision` still matches `submitted_revision`, the submit did not complete — safe to re-drive. If `current_revision` has changed, the stack was rebased externally — do NOT re-drive, escalate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `phase` enum vocabulary (all valid values and their meaning) co-located with the `recovery.json` schema in the Drain AC, or add a normative cross-reference to the S2 plan section that defines them.
findings.finding: The `phase` enum value vocabulary used in `recovery.json` is referenced but never defined in this plan. AC3 introduces `paused` as a phase value; AC5 names `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`, and `mid-handback` as kill-points that write a `phase` field into recovery.json. The full set of valid values and their semantics are not co-located with the recovery.json schema here, nor is a normative cross-reference given. An executor implementing the startup reconciliation must know all valid `phase` values to write a correct dispatch table.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery (recovery.json schema); AC: Crash-recovery/idempotency test
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table to the Drain+recovery AC: `phase` ∈ {`idle`, `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`, `submitting_handback`, `paused`} with a one-line description of each, so the recovery dispatch table is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `re_drive_count` in `recovery.json` is read back and accumulated on restart (to enforce the per-stack re-drive cap across restarts) or reset, and state the rationale.
findings.finding: The `re_drive_count` field is listed as part of the `recovery.json` schema but no protocol specifies how the restarted bot uses it. It is unclear whether the restarted bot: (a) reads it back and uses it to gate further re-drives against a cap, (b) resets it to zero, or (c) treats it as audit-only. This is an ambiguous counter placement that could cause a crash-recovery loop to bypass the re-drive cap S2 establishes.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery (recovery.json schema)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'On recovery, the restarted bot restores `re_drive_count` from `recovery.json` and continues counting against the same cap — not reset to zero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback to the Drain+recovery AC for a corrupt/unparseable `recovery.json`: specify whether the bot treats it as absent (logs alarm, discards, proceeds normally) or halts for operator inspection.
findings.finding: No fallback behavior is specified for the case where `recovery.json` exists but is corrupt or truncated (e.g., from a SIGKILL mid-write after the SIGTERM grace period expires). The plan specifies how the file is written and read, but an implementer has no guidance on what the bot should do if JSON parsing fails.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'If `recovery.json` fails to parse, the bot logs an `autolander_errors` alarm event, deletes the file, and falls through to normal work selection (safe: the worst case is a re-drive of an already-submitted stack, which Gerrit's idempotent submit rejects cleanly).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Drain+recovery AC which source of truth is authoritative for handback state on recovery (S3 marker or recovery.json.acknowledged), and specify the decision rule when they disagree.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field in `recovery.json` and S3's `acknowledged` marker are both referenced in the mid-handback recovery rule, but the plan does not specify the relationship between them or what to do if they are inconsistent. Specifically: if `recovery.json.acknowledged=false` but S3's marker shows `acknowledged=true` (written before the crash), the correct action (skip handback; clean up recovery.json) is not stated.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'S3's marker is authoritative; if S3 shows `acknowledged=true`, skip handback regardless of `recovery.json.acknowledged`. `recovery.json.acknowledged` is a hint for the fast path only.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly which threshold (or a distinct third value, e.g. 45 s) the HEALTHCHECK uses in AC4, and give it a named constant so both AC4 and AC5 reference the same symbol.
findings.finding: AC4 references 'the staleness threshold' to define when HEALTHCHECK marks the container unhealthy, but AC5 defines two distinct threshold values (freshness ≤ 15 s for normal operation; > 90 s for lander_down) and neither is labeled 'the staleness threshold.' An implementer must guess which value the HEALTHCHECK should use.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) and AC5 (Heartbeat)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a named constant, e.g. `HEARTBEAT_STALE_S = 45` (3× poll), and rewrite AC4 as 'unhealthy if heartbeat file age > HEARTBEAT_STALE_S (45 s)' to sit between the write cadence (15 s) and the lander_down threshold (90 s).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete SIGTERM drain deadline (e.g., `SIGTERM_DRAIN_DEADLINE_S = 300`) in the plan rather than '~ the CI-wait timeout', or add a cross-reference to the specific config variable that holds it.
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline is specified as '~ the CI-wait timeout', which is approximate and requires the executor to determine what CI-wait timeout value to use; no concrete duration (e.g., 300 s) is given.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '~ the CI-wait timeout' with a concrete value such as 'bounded to 300 s (matching the S2 CI-wait timeout defined in `config.py`)' and name the config variable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `recovery.json` is written atomically (write to a `.tmp` file, then `os.replace()`) so a SIGKILL mid-write leaves the prior clean state, not a corrupted file.
findings.finding: The `recovery.json` write is not specified as atomic. If the process receives SIGKILL (e.g., Docker's post-grace-period hard-kill) mid-write, the file is left partially written/corrupted; on restart the reconciliation reads a corrupted file and the recovery guarantee fails.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'writes a concrete recovery record (recovery.json in the state volume)'
findings.suggested_fix: Write to `recovery.json.tmp`, flush+fsync, then `os.replace('recovery.json.tmp', 'recovery.json')`. On startup, if only the `.tmp` exists, treat it as no recovery record (prior state unknown → safe fallback).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements for drain/recovery: specify that drain-timeout events, recovery.json writes, and reconciliation outcomes (ack, re-drive, error) are emitted as structured log entries and, for the timeout/error cases, as an alertable metric or status-endpoint field.
findings.finding: Failure modes during drain and startup reconciliation (timeout hit before drain completes, recovery.json write failure, reconciliation error on restart) are not instrumented with any metric, log, or alert — operators have no signal that recovery is in a degraded or failed state.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery (entire AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation deliverable: an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the recovery.json schema, the drain state machine, the reconciliation algorithm, and the rationale for key thresholds (90 s, SIGTERM deadline).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel crash-safe recovery pattern (recovery.json, bounded drain, startup reconciliation) that spans loop.py, S3, Gerrit, and the status endpoint — a significant architectural decision — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is specified.
findings.location: Files section and AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/autolander/failure.py` to S5d's Files section as READ (the recovery reconciliation in loop.py consumes its marker schema) and explicitly name the fields it depends on (`acknowledged`, `change_id`, `chain_member_ids`, `tested_shas`, `phase`).
findings.finding: `infra/autolander/failure.py` (created by S3, `darksome-starry-sable`) is not enumerated in S5d's Files section, yet S5d's drain+crash-safe recovery AC explicitly reads and reconciles against S3's marker format (`acknowledged=false`, `change_id`, `chain_member_ids`, `tested_shas`). The recovery reconciliation logic in loop.py is a direct consumer of failure.py's output schema — a cross-file contract that is not acknowledged in the Files list.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'the bot reads `recovery.json` + reconciles against LIVE Gerrit and S3's markers BEFORE selecting new work'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the autolander state volume MUST be declared `external: true` in docker-compose.yml as a bind-mount onto the EBS data volume (e.g. `/var/gerrit/site/autolander`), added to `SITE_SUBDIRS` in compose-up.sh, and thus covered by DLM daily snapshots — matching the pattern used for all other stateful Gerrit subdirs.
findings.finding: The 'state volume' holding recovery.json and the emergency-stop sentinel is never specified as external:true (EBS-bound, protected from docker compose down -v) or confirmed as covered by DLM daily snapshots. If the volume is a plain named volume rather than an external bind-to-EBS volume, a docker compose down -v or a container recreation destroys the crash recovery record, defeating the stated RELIABILITY FALLBACK guarantee.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery (RELIABILITY FALLBACK) — 'recovery.json in the state volume'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add: `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml — EDIT: add autolander_state external:true volume` and `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh — EDIT: append 'autolander' to SITE_SUBDIRS`. In the drain-on-shutdown AC, insert: 'The state volume MUST be external:true, bind-mounted onto /var/gerrit/site/autolander on the EBS data volume, so recovery.json survives docker compose down -v and is included in DLM daily snapshots.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the heartbeat file (e.g., `/state/heartbeat`) and the sentinel file (e.g., `/state/emergency_stop`) explicitly in the plan so all components (bot, HEALTHCHECK, S5c endpoint, operator runbooks) use the same paths.
findings.finding: The heartbeat file path and the emergency-stop sentinel file path are never named; the executor must choose filenames, creating inconsistency risk between the bot, HEALTHCHECK, S5c status endpoint, and operator runbooks.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat) — 'timestamp file in the state volume'; Emergency-stop AC — 'sentinel file in the state volume'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'State-volume layout' section listing: `heartbeat` (written by bot, stat'd by HEALTHCHECK, served by S5c), `emergency_stop` (presence = paused), `recovery.json` (written on drain deadline).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a timeout and retry budget (with backoff) for the startup reconciliation queries to Gerrit and S3, and define the fallback behaviour (e.g., after N retries, log an alert and either park the recovery record for manual review or proceed without new work selection).
findings.finding: The reconciliation step on restart queries live Gerrit and S3, but the plan specifies no timeout, retry budget, or degraded-mode fallback for the case where those services are down at restart time — leaving the bot unable to start new work indefinitely.
findings.location: AC: Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'On restart the bot reads recovery.json + reconciles against LIVE Gerrit and S3's markers BEFORE selecting new work'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define POLL_INTERVAL (and derived thresholds like heartbeat staleness = 6× POLL_INTERVAL) as a single configurable constant (env var or config file) referenced by loop.py, the HEALTHCHECK command, and the `land` endpoint — not duplicated as magic numbers.
findings.finding: The 90 s liveness threshold and 15 s poll cadence are hardcoded in the spec with no mention of configurability; if the poll cadence changes (e.g., tuned in S2), every dependent constant (heartbeat write interval, staleness threshold, HEALTHCHECK interval) must be found and updated manually.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat): 'freshness ≤ 15 s; lander_down treats age > 90 s (6× poll) as down'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `POLL_INTERVAL_S` as an env var (default 15); compute `HEARTBEAT_STALE_S = 6 * POLL_INTERVAL_S` in one place and pass it to both the HEALTHCHECK and the `land` endpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the heartbeat file is written atomically (write to a `.tmp` file then `os.replace()`) to prevent the HEALTHCHECK from observing a partial or zero-byte file mid-write.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the write mechanism (atomic rename vs. direct overwrite) for the heartbeat timestamp file. A direct in-place write can produce a partially-written or zero-byte file visible between open() and close(), causing the HEALTHCHECK's stat to race with the write and report a spurious stale-file result, triggering an unwarranted autoheal restart.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat) and AC4 (Liveness/restart) — heartbeat file write in loop.py
findings.suggested_fix: In loop.py, write the timestamp to `heartbeat.tmp` then call `os.replace('heartbeat.tmp', 'heartbeat')` — this is an atomic rename on POSIX and eliminates the partial-write window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `infra/autolander/loop.py` does not yet exist: change the Files label from EDIT to CREATE (or NEW), or explicitly name the prerequisite ticket that creates it and declare a hard dependency.
findings.finding: `infra/autolander/loop.py` is labeled 'EDIT' in the Files section — implying the file already exists — but no `infra/autolander/` directory or `loop.py` file exists anywhere in the repo.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/autolander/loop.py` — EDIT
findings.suggested_fix: Change `infra/autolander/loop.py — EDIT` to `infra/autolander/loop.py — CREATE (depends on S2a/S5a landing first)` and add an explicit `deps:` entry referencing the ticket that creates the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add S5c as a declared hard dependency of S5d (or note that AC5's contract clause is only verifiable after S5c lands), so the ordering is explicit and the executor doesn't treat the endpoint as pre-existing.
findings.finding: AC5 asserts that `heartbeat_age_s` 'is served by S5c's status endpoint' as a live contract this plan's heartbeat-write relies on — but S5c (ticket 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) is still `open` and the status endpoint does not yet exist in the codebase.
findings.location: AC5 Heartbeat — 'its age is served as `heartbeat_age_s` by S5c's status endpoint'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and link the ticket that creates the S3-marker read/write infrastructure, or specify the exact file path, function signature, and schema of the marker this plan will use/create, so the dependency is traceable and the recovery logic is implementable.
findings.finding: The drain/recovery AC asserts the bot 'writes its outcome to S3's marker' on SIGTERM and that recovery reconciles against 'S3's markers', treating S3-marker infrastructure as pre-existing — but no S3 marker module, path, or write function exists anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: AC — Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery — 'writes its outcome to S3's marker'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify the `land`/`lander_down` clause in AC5 as a forward-contract (not a current-binding) by noting it is defined by a future ticket, or add an explicit dependency on the ticket that builds the `land` service.
findings.finding: AC5 references "`land`'s `lander_down` treats age **> 90 s** as down" as a live consumer contract — but no `land` service or `lander_down` binding exists anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: AC5 Heartbeat — '`land`'s `lander_down` treats age > 90 s as down'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale explaining why the HEALTHCHECK stats the local timestamp file rather than curling the S5c status endpoint: the file is written by the event-loop body itself, so a hung event loop stops updating it even if the HTTP status-server thread remains responsive.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the timestamp-file-on-state-volume approach for heartbeat over the already-planned S5c loopback status endpoint. Without this, an implementer may question why two mechanisms coexist and whether the file write is redundant with the endpoint's `heartbeat_age_s`.
findings.location: AC: Heartbeat (AC5) and Liveness/restart (AC4)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the willfarrell/autoheal image to a specific digest or semver tag (e.g. `willfarrell/autoheal:v1.2.0`) in the compose service definition, consistent with how gerrit and reviewbot images are pinned in the existing stack.
findings.finding: The plan names `willfarrell/autoheal` as the sidecar image but specifies no image tag. The willfarrell/autoheal project has made breaking label-key changes across major versions (e.g. stop-timeout label changed in v0.9). Using an untagged (implicit `latest`) image in a production compose file means a future `docker compose pull` could silently pull a breaking version that ignores `autoheal=true` or changes restart behavior.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart): 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (S5a) restarts the `autoheal=true`-labeled container on `unhealthy`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
